anonExecutableSecurityDefiner.rationale: public.get_customer_phone_otp_capabilities() takes no arguments, performs no table reads or writes, and returns exactly two booleans derived from app.platform_feature_enabled() feature flags (sms/whatsapp capability). It is a deliberate public capability-discovery endpoint with no PII exposure and cannot be used as a per-customer oracle since it takes no input. Verified via pg_get_functiondef against production.

authenticatedExecutableSecurityDefiner.rationale: This is the platform's deliberate architecture: every module writes through a SECURITY DEFINER RPC callable by 'authenticated' that is expected to check tenant/staff/role ownership internally (RLS alone cannot express all of these checks, e.g. cross-table tenant joins). A catalog read proves each function has EXECUTE granted to authenticated and a pinned search_path (verified: 0 of 253 SECURITY DEFINER functions across public+app lack a pinned search_path) -- it cannot prove the function body correctly enforces tenant ownership at runtime. That runtime proof is exactly what launch-blockers.json's P0-RLS-GRANTS-004 (db/tests/rls_adversarial_isolation.sql, run with real SET ROLE / request.jwt.claims principals) exists to close. Disposition: architecturally accepted as a class, launch-blocking as individual runtime-unverified instances until that suite runs against production. Not a new/duplicate finding relative to P0-RLS-GRANTS-004 -- this warning set IS what that gate covers.

searchPathAudit.rationale: 0 of 253 SECURITY DEFINER functions in public+app lack an explicit search_path in proconfig -- the classic search-path-hijack class is closed fleet-wide.

publicPseudoRoleExecuteAudit.rationale: All 7 are non-SECURITY-DEFINER app-schema helpers: two are trigger functions (forbid_mutation, touch_updated_at) which Postgres invokes without checking the invoking role's EXECUTE privilege at all, so the grant is inert; the rest (norm_phone, phone_match_key, role_class, role_perms, sale_policy_defaults) are pure/stateless computations with no table access, safe under any caller. The app schema is not the PostgREST-exposed API schema (only public is), so none of these are reachable via REST regardless.

unindexedForeignKeys.currentRealtimeRisk: LOW -- production is pre-launch; busiest sampled table (sales) estimates 10 rows
unindexedForeignKeys.scaleRisk: The real risk is workload-shape, not current row count: financial/ledger and booking hot-path tables will be joined constantly once real tenants land.
unindexedForeignKeys.recommendation: Create covering indexes for the unindexed FK columns on the PRE-LAUNCH tier before onboarding the first real tenant (these are exactly the tables named in P0-FINANCE-REVERSAL-005 and P0-REPORTING-SCALE-006's own success criteria). Defer the rest until real usage data shows which actually get joined -- creating an index nobody queries by just adds write overhead, and Supabase's own 'unused index' advisor (below) already shows the cost of guessing wrong pre-launch.

multiplePermissivePolicies.rationale: 83 of 87 are the deliberate v14 super-admin '<table>_sa_read' policy sitting alongside each table's normal tenant policy for the same role+action (matches CLAUDE.md's documented '46 SELECT-only <t>_sa_read policies' design). This is a real, measurable-at-scale query-planner cost (Postgres evaluates both permissive predicates per row) but zero security risk. 4 are a different, consolidatable owner_read/owner_write redundancy on client_field_definitions, client_field_options, client_field_values, and subscriptions.

unusedIndexes.rationale: Every one of these indexes supports a query shape the product will need once it has real traffic (payroll/commission reports, branch-scoped cash-drawer lookups, actionable-wallet filters, membership status lookups) -- they are unused because the database has near-zero rows and no real usage yet, not because they are unnecessary. Dropping indexes pre-launch on the strength of a zero-traffic advisor reading would be premature; revisit after the first real usage window (tie this review to the P0-POST-CUTOVER-SMOKE-016 monitoring window).

authConnectionStrategy.rationale: Auth's DB connection pool is configured as an absolute count (10) rather than a percentage of the instance's connection budget; harmless at current near-zero load, but should be switched to percentage-based before increasing compute size, per Supabase's own guidance, or a future resize silently won't help Auth throughput.
